themes.name: Dead People, Great Problems
themes.tagline: Ancient generals, Roman lawyers, and medieval siege engines — history's most dramatic overachievers are back to teach us things.

items.title: Cicero In and On Political Crises - Melissa Lane
items.description:
00:00 // Introduction: Meet Professor Melissa Lane 
00:15 // Cicero's Dilemma: A Letter on Tyranny

05:36 // Philosopher and Statesman of Rome 
09:44 // Five Crises That Defined Cicero's Life 
12:27 // Crisis One: The Gracchi and Land Reform 
18:39 // Crisis Two: Sulla's Dictatorship and Civil War 
23:16 // Crisis Three: The Catiline Conspiracy

items.title: Trebuchet!
items.channel_title: The History Guy: History Deserves to Be Remembered
items.description:
On July 24, 1304 King Edward I of England captured the last stronghold of resistance against English rule in Scotland, the formidable Stirling Castle. But he did  not have to storm the castle to make the defenders surrender. He merely had to threaten them with perhaps the most terrifying siege engine in the era before gunpowder. 

Check out our new shop for fun The History Guy merchandise:
https://thehistoryguy-shop.fourthwall.com/products/thg-history

This is original content based on research

items.title: The Rise and Fall of Ancient Greece's Themistocles
items.description:
A legend of the great Greek city of Athens, Themistocles rose from obscurity to save ancient Greece and helped shape one of the greatest naval powers in history. Yet his story ends in exile, condemned as a traitor and serving the very empire he once defeated: Persia.

In this episode of The Ancients, Tristan Hughes is joined by Michael Scott to explore the extraordinary life of Themistocles. Together Tristan and Michael delve into the political intrigue of early Athenian democracy, charting Them

items.title: The Rules of Foot Combat | Knights' Tournaments in the 16th Century
items.description:
When we think of Tudor tournaments, we tend to think of jousting. But foot combat was an essential element of the tournament and also necessary practice for warfare. Foot combat encompassed a number of sports on foot, such as wrestling or sword fighting; combat without a horse. They were dangerous events, undertaken by strong and fit knights, often in heavy armour, and something we know Henry VIII relished. 

Image credits:
Henry VIII (1491-1547) c.1560-80 © Royal Collection Enterprises Ltd 2025

morning_soundtrack.title: Dalia Stasevska shines a light on Ukraine's neglected composers on new album
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Dalia Stasevska is a rising star in the conducting world. On her new album, "Ukrainian Mixtape," she's promoting some of Ukraine's neglected composers.
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Like discovering a hidden room full of candlelight and forgotten masterpieces, each note a small act of reclamation.

morning_soundtrack.title: Joy Oladokun Announces New Album Hope Is A Heavy Thing: Hear “Kind”
morning_soundtrack.snippet:
At the top of this year, Nashville-based singer-songwriter Joy Oladokun released a very moving single "Nothing Comes Easy." Today, she's returned with another thoughtful single "Kind," which also announces her new album Hope Is A Heavy Thing that's out September 25. 
The post Joy Oladokun Announces
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The kind of song you put on when you're holding yourself together with both hands and sheer stubbornness.

morning_soundtrack.snippet: Following a successful broadway stint as the Emcee in Cabaret last year, country pop star Orville Peck is returning as the masked balladeer with a new album. Mule, which is out September 18 via Warner Records, will be his latest full-length since 2022's Bronco. He announced news with the lead single
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Lonesome highway at dusk, the kind of ache that looks good in a sequined mask.

morning_soundtrack.title: Jack White Covers Free With The Black Crowes’ Chris Robinson
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Years ago, one of my editors made a point about Jack White that I've had in my head ever since: He's Robert Plant and Jimmy Page at the same time. Within the White Stripes, White was more or less forced to play that role. This was a bare-bones duo who wanted to achieve rock-star immortality, so Whit
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Two guitarists channeling decades of raw rock swagger into a sweat-soaked room that probably smelled like leather and amplifier heat.

morning_soundtrack.title: BONUS TRACKS: Raul Malo's Final Performance, New Music from Buddy Miller, Folk Alley's Cover Contest, and More Roots Music News
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Today marks the release of a double LP of The Mavericks' late frontman Raul Malo's final performance. The posthumous new album, BELLA CIAO: RAUL MALO Live In Nashville, was recorded last Father's Day weekend at the 3rd & Lindsley club with his son Dino
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: A bittersweet last dance under the neon of a club that feels smaller than it should, carrying the weight of a final goodbye.

morning_soundtrack.title: ALBUM REVIEW: The Revivalists Explore Difficult Issues Via Catchy Sounds With 'Get It Honest'
morning_soundtrack.snippet: With their sixth album, Get It Honest, the New Orleans-based octet The Revivalists continue to mine bluesy, classic, and Southern-rock templates. Produced by Rich Costey — who also helmed their last album, 2023’s Pour It Out into the Night — Get It Honest reiterates the band&
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Southern rock with its sleeves rolled up, asking hard questions over a cold beer on a humid evening porch.

morning_soundtrack.title: Hole Reissuing Classic Albums on Vinyl
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Pretty on the Inside, Live Through This, and Celebrity Skin are getting an array of wax represses
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The crackling electricity of putting on a record that still makes you feel dangerous twenty years later.

A filthy blues cover performed at White's show in Chicago.

The lead single, "Too Little, Too Late," is out now.

global_silver_linings.title: Data Centers Had Been Benefitting from Sales Tax Breaks: More and More States Are Revoking Them
global_silver_linings.snippet: As the AI infrastructure build out continues to meet headwinds from concerned citizens, 3 states recently signed laws ending what had been the largely standard practice of exempting data center operators from sales taxes. Tucked into a state budget passed on June 15th, Arizona Governor Katie Hobbs touted that data centers located in the state […] The post Data Centers Had Been Benefitting from Sales Tax Breaks: More and More States Are Revoking Them appeared first on Good News Network .
global_silver_linings.reason: States are reclaiming public funds that were quietly flowing to some of the world's wealthiest tech companies, redirecting that tax revenue toward schools, infrastructure, and community services. It's a quiet but meaningful correction in who actually benefits from public policy.

global_silver_linings.title: When It Comes to Addiction Treatment, ‘Any Door Should Be the Right Door’
global_silver_linings.snippet: This article was originally published by the Daily Yonder, which is dedicated to uplifting the stories of rural people. Holly Warren was recently leafing through one of her old medical school textbooks, trying to recall how much instruction she’d received in addiction medicine — trying to recall, “Did we even talk about this? Was...
global_silver_linings.reason: A growing movement in addiction care means that wherever someone finds the courage to ask for help — a clinic, an ER, a community center — they can receive it without being turned away or bounced between systems. Meeting people exactly where they are saves lives, and that principle is finally gaining real traction.

discovery_articles.title: Tuam Railway Station in Tuam, Ireland
discovery_articles.snippet: Tuam Railway Station opened in 1860 as part of the Waterford, Limerick and Western Railway, becoming one of the most important railway hubs in the west of Ireland. Passenger services ended in 1976 as part of a nationwide rationalisation of the railway network, although freight trains continued to use the station until 1993. Since then, the station has stood largely abandoned, with its Victorian buildings gradually falling into disrepair.

discovery_articles.snippet: Archaeology students at the University of Florida discovered the rare vessel at the site of the former Mission San Francisco de Potano, established in 1606 when Florida was under Spanish rule

discovery_articles.title: Why Are These Whales Floating Upside-Down? They Might Just Be Moms Who Need a Break From Feeding Their Babies
discovery_articles.snippet: Each year, southern right whales migrate thousands of miles from their feeding grounds to breed, give birth and raise their young. Taking rests and regulating their calves' nursing schedules might help mothers conserve energy while they're far from food

discovery_articles.snippet: A chat with aged avatars of future selves can guide people paralyzed by tough decisions. The question is what information to feed the bots.

discovery_articles.title: What kinds of black holes exist — and how are they different?
discovery_articles.snippet: Scientists have identified four types of black holes, classified mostly by their masses. But these mysterious objects continue to challenge our understanding of the universe.

featured_read.title: Albert Camus on the Three Antidotes to the Absurdity of Life
featured_read.snippet: "In a world whose absurdity appears to be so impenetrable, we simply must reach a greater degree of understanding among men, a greater sincerity."
featured_read.blurb: Camus believed that staring down life's fundamental meaninglessness doesn't have to end in despair — and this piece traces his quietly radical argument that sincerity, rebellion, and human solidarity are not consolations but actual answers. Set aside the half-hour and let it rearrange something.

garden_note.note: The long July evening stretches luxuriously past nine, and Zürich's gardens are deep in their most generous mood — beans dangling heavy on their poles, dahlias beginning their slow theatrical ascent toward August glory.
in_season: French beans (Buschbohnen) — harvest daily to keep plants producing
Dahlias — stake and disbud for showier blooms
Zucchini — check under leaves; they hide and swell overnight
Lavender — at peak, cut before it fully browns for drying
garden_note.sky_tonight: A waxing gibbous moon rises in the southeast after dusk — bright enough to cast faint shadows across the beds, with Saturn climbing not far behind it later in the night.
garden_note.sun_times: The sun slips away at 21:12 tonight, with true dusk lingering until 21:47 — take a slow walk around the garden in that golden gap.

puzzle.label: Fern's Morning Riddle
puzzle.prompt: I may be simple, I may be complex; I may have a name, but no gender or sex; I am often a question, or statement as a setup; I tend to have an answer, 'til you find it I won't let up. What am I?

previous_puzzle.label: Fern's Morning Anagram
previous_puzzle.prompt: Rearrange the letters of EARTH to make a different common word.

fern_data.greeting: The kettle's going, the lamp is doing its amber thing against the window, and somewhere in tonight's pile Cicero is having a worse political crisis than yours — which helps — while ants are quietly outscoring humans on intelligence tests and a YouTuber ten years in is reckoning with what the algorithm has cost him.
